policy_statement: Prepare a target programme for using renewable energy in City construction projects and existing buildings and monitor progress.
mechanism_description: The City will prepare a target programme to increase renewable energy use in construction projects and existing buildings and will monitor progress, which helps displace fossil energy use.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy supply mix shift → ↓ fossil fuel combustion for heat/electricity → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: City-led programmes/targets increase renewable energy integration in new construction and existing buildings (e.g., solar PV/solar thermal, renewable district heating, renewable electricity procurement). (2) Process: Renewable generation or renewable-sourced heat/electricity substitutes for fossil-based energy in building operations, lowering combustion and upstream fossil generation. (3) Outcome: Reduced operational CO₂e emissions from buildings (and associated grid/district energy), contributing to climate change mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from building energy use by replacing fossil electricity/heat with renewable energy.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Increasing renewable energy use in buildings primarily works by displacing fossil energy and cutting GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although programs can include efficiency or cost-saving elements, the defining mechanism is fuel switching/supply decarbonization, which is a direct mitigation pathway rather than “same service → less energy input” (Resource Efficiency) or hazard risk reduction (Adaptation).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program were purely about reducing energy demand (without renewables) it would shift toward Resource Efficiency; if framed as backup power for outages it could include Adaptation co-benefits. Here, the described causal chain is explicitly renewable uptake to displace fossil energy.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy matching “renewable energy program in buildings → fossil energy displacement” has its main climate effect through emissions reductions from cleaner energy supply, so Mitigation applies across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Any further sub-classification (e.g., on-site PV vs renewable district heat vs green power procurement) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Programs that merely set targets/monitoring without implementation may have uncertain realized impact, but the intended dominant pathway remains mitigation via fossil displacement; co-benefits (energy security/resilience, air quality) do not change the primary category.
key_indicators: "target programme"; "using renewable energy"; "City's construction projects and existing buildings"; "will be prepared"; "progress ... will be monitored"
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a planning/strategy instrument (“programme … will be prepared”), but the explicit “target programme” language makes target-setting the best fit. Monitoring is mentioned but not framed as a standalone disclosure/benchmarking requirement.
